[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgoin Temple|ja|聖護院}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)}} in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to {{ill|Gojō Street|ja|五条通}} and then across the river to {{ill|Teramachi Street|ja|寺町通}}, engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as {{ill|Kawaramachi Street|ja|河原町通}}, {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Street|ja|大宮通}}, and {{ill|Senbon Street|ja|千本通}}; and south to {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺}}, {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺}}, and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the {{ill|Sentō Imperial Palace|ja|仙洞御所}}, the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the {{ill|Kyoto Machibugyō|ja|京都町奉行}} offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgoin Temple|ja|聖護院}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)|12=draft}} in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|12=draft}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to {{ill|Gojō Street|ja|五条通|12=draft}} and then across the river to {{ill|Teramachi Street|ja|寺町通|12=draft}}, engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as {{ill|Kawaramachi Street|ja|河原町通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|12=draft}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|12=draft}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Street|ja|大宮通|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Senbon Street|ja|千本通|12=draft}}; and south to {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|12=draft}}, and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the {{ill|Sentō Imperial Palace|ja|仙洞御所|12=draft}}, the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the {{ill|Kyoto Machibugyō|ja|京都町奉行|12=draft}} offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|12=draft}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgoin Temple|ja|聖護院|12=draft}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院|12=draft}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|12=draft}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中|12=draft}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉|12=draft}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=draft}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=draft}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=draft}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

{{draft categories| [[Category:1788 in Japan]] [[Category:1788 disasters]] [[Category:Kyoto in the Edo period]] [[Category:Fires in the Edo period]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tenmei Fire]] [[Category:Teramachi]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Tenmei Fire]] [[ja:天明の大火]] [[en:Tenmei Fire]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tenmei Fire}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=draft}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)|12=draft}} in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|12=draft}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to {{ill|Gojō Street|ja|五条通|12=draft}} and then across the river to {{ill|Teramachi Street|ja|寺町通|12=draft}}, engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as {{ill|Kawaramachi Street|ja|河原町通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|12=draft}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|12=draft}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Street|ja|大宮通|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Senbon Street|ja|千本通|12=draft}}; and south to {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|12=draft}}, and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the {{ill|Sentō Imperial Palace|ja|仙洞御所|12=draft}}, the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the {{ill|Kyoto Machibugyō|ja|京都町奉行|12=draft}} offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|12=draft}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgoin Temple|ja|聖護院|12=draft}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院|12=draft}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|12=draft}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中|12=draft}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉|12=draft}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=draft}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=draft}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015年10月}} '''天明の大火'''（てんめいの たいか）とは、[[天明]]8年[[1月30日 (旧暦)|1月30日]]（[[1788年]][[3月7日]]）に[[京都]]で発生した[[火災]]。出火場所の名をとって'''団栗焼け'''（どんぐりやけ）、また干支から'''申年の大火'''（さるどしの たいか）とも呼ばれた。単に'''京都大火'''（きょうとたいか）あるいは'''都焼け'''（みやこやけ）というと、通常はこの天明の大火のことを指す。
京都で発生した史上最大規模の火災で、[[京都御所|御所]]・[[二条城]]・[[京都所司代]]などの要所を軒並み焼失したほか、当時の京都市街の8割以上が灰燼に帰した。被害は京都を焼け野原にした[[応仁の乱]]の戦火による焼亡をさらに上回るものとなり、その後の京都の経済にも深刻な打撃を与えた。[[江戸時代]]の京都はこの前後にも[[宝永の大火]]と[[元治]]の[[どんどん焼け]]<!--（[[禁門の変]]に伴うもの）--><!--詳述はリンク先に-->で市街の多くを焼失しており、これらを「京都の三大大火」と呼ぶこともある<ref>『[[国史大辞典_(昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]』第9巻（吉川弘文館、1988年）「天明の京都大火」（執筆者:森谷尅久）</ref>。
== 概要 == 1月30日（3月7日）<!--（当時の感覚でいえば、--><!--感覚の問題ではなく、それが暦だったのです--><!--現代の暦ではあくまでも1月31日が「正月晦日」だと思うのですが-->の未明、[[鴨川 (淀川水系)|鴨川]]東側の宮川町団栗辻子（現在の[[京都市]][[東山区]]宮川筋付近）の[[町屋 (商家)|町家]]から出火。空き家への放火だったという。折からの強風に煽られて瞬く間に南は[[五条通]]にまで達し、更に火の粉が鴨川対岸の[[寺町通]]に燃え移って洛中に延焼した。その日の夕方には二条城本丸が炎上し、続いて洛中北部の御所にも燃え移った。最終的な鎮火は発生から2日後の2月2日（3月9日）早朝のことだった。
この火災で東は[[河原町通|河原町]]・[[木屋町]]・[[大和大路]]まで、北は[[上御霊神社]]・[[鞍馬口通]]・[[今宮御旅所]]まで、西は[[智恵光院通]]・[[大宮通]]・[[千本通]]まで、南は[[東本願寺]]・[[西本願寺]]・[[六条通]]まで達し、御所・二条城のみならず、[[仙洞御所]]・京都所司代屋敷・[[京都町奉行|東西両奉行所]]・[[摂関家]]の邸宅も焼失した。幕府公式の「罹災記録」（京都[[町代]]を務めた古久保家の記録）によれば、京都市中1967町のうち焼失した町は1424、焼失家屋は3万6797、焼失世帯6万5340、焼失寺院201、焼失神社37、死者150だったという{{R|F1}}。ただし死者に関しては公式記録の値引きが疑われ、実際の死者は1800はあったとする説もある{{R|F1}}。 [[光格天皇]]は御所が再建されるまでの3年間、[[聖護院]]を[[行宮]]（仮御所）とし、[[恭礼門院]]は[[妙法院]]、[[後桜町天皇|後桜町上皇]]は[[青蓮院]]（[[青蓮院|粟田御所]]）にそれぞれ移った。後桜町院の生母青綺門院の仮御所となった[[知恩院]]と青蓮院の間に、幕府が廊下を設けて通行の便を図っている。
この大火に[[江戸幕府]]も衝撃を受け、急遽[[老中]]で幕閣の中心人物であった[[松平定信]]を京都に派遣して[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]と善後策を協議した。また、この直後に[[裏松固禅]]の『[[大内裏図考證]]』が完成し、その研究に基づいて焼失した内裏の再建は古式に則った形式で行われることとなった。再建の費用は幕府から出資された。これは幕府の慢性的な財政難と[[天明の大飢饉]]における民衆の苦しみを理由に、かつてのような古式に則った壮麗な御所は建てることはできない、とする松平定信の反対論を押し切ったものであり、憤慨した定信は[[京都所司代]]や[[京都町奉行]]に対して朝廷の新規の要求には応じてはならないと指示している（[[東京大学史料編纂所]]所蔵「御所々御入用筋書抜」四及び松平定信『[[宇下人言]]』）<ref>藤田覚『近世政治史と天皇』（吉川弘文館、1999年） ISBN 978-4-642-03353-4 第四章「寛政内裏造営をめぐる朝幕関係」</ref>。これにより「幕府」に対する「朝廷」の動向が世間の注目を浴びるようになり、さらに「[[尊号一件]]」などの幕府と朝廷間の紛争の遠因となった。
天明の大火から10年後の、寛政10年7月1日（1798年8月12日）から翌2日にかけて、京都[[方広寺]]大仏（[[京の大仏]]）・大仏殿が落雷による火災のため全焼した<ref>[[村山修一]]『京都大仏御殿盛衰記』法藏館、2003年</ref>。方広寺大仏殿は当時日本最大の木造建築であり、火の粉によって京都市街に火災が広がり、天明の大火のようになる恐れもあったが、奇跡的に市街へ燃え広がらずに済んだ。
== 出典 == {{Reflist|2 |refs= <ref name="F1">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A9%E6%98%8E%E3%81%AE%E5%A4%A7%E7%81%AB-578997#goog_rewarded 天明の大火 (てんめいのたいか)][[コトバンク]]</ref> }}
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.adthree.com/NPO/tenmeitaika.html 天明の大火による焼亡域]（NPO 災害から文化財を守る会）
{{デフォルトソート:てんめいのたいか}} [[Category:1788年の日本]] [[Category:1788年の災害]] [[Category:江戸時代の京都]] [[Category:江戸時代の火災]]

{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to {{ill|Gojō Street|ja|五条通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and then across the river to {{ill|Teramachi Street|ja|寺町通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as {{ill|Kawaramachi Street|ja|河原町通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Street|ja|大宮通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Senbon Street|ja|千本通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; and south to {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the {{ill|Sentō Imperial Palace|ja|仙洞御所|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the {{ill|Kyoto Machibugyō|ja|京都町奉行|qq=draft|qq=draft}} offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgo-in Temple|ja|聖護院|1=Shōgo-in|lt=Shōgo-in Temple|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to {{ill|Gojō Street|ja|五条通|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and then across the river to {{ill|Teramachi Street|ja|寺町通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as {{ill|Kawaramachi Street|ja|河原町通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Street|ja|大宮通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Senbon Street|ja|千本通|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; and south to {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the {{ill|Sentō Imperial Palace|ja|仙洞御所|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the {{ill|Kyoto Machibugyō|ja|京都町奉行|qq=draft|qq=draft}} offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgo-in Temple|ja|聖護院|1=Shōgo-in|lt=Shōgo-in Temple|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the {{ill|Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Yodo River system)|qq=}} in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to {{ill|Gojō Street|ja|五条通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Street|qq=}} and then across the river to {{ill|Teramachi Street|ja|寺町通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teramachi Street|qq=}}, engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as {{ill|Kawaramachi Street|ja|河原町通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawaramachi Street|qq=}}, {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Street|ja|大宮通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Senbon Street|ja|千本通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senbon Street|qq=}}; and south to {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Honganji|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Honganji|qq=}}, and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the {{ill|Sentō Imperial Palace|ja|仙洞御所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sentō Imperial Palace|qq=}}, the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the {{ill|Kyoto Machibugyō|ja|京都町奉行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Machibugyō|qq=}} offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five regent houses|qq=}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgo-in Temple|ja|聖護院|1=Shōgo-in|lt=Shōgo-in Temple|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgo-in Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgo-in|qq=}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōren-in|qq=}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chion-in|qq=}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rōjū|qq=}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmei famine|qq=}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to {{ill|Gojō Street|ja|五条通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Street|qq=}} and then across the river to {{ill|Teramachi Street|ja|寺町通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teramachi Street|qq=}}, engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as {{ill|Kawaramachi Street|ja|河原町通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawaramachi Street|qq=}}, {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Street|ja|大宮通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Senbon Street|ja|千本通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senbon Street|qq=}}; and south to {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Honganji|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Honganji|qq=}}, and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the {{ill|Sentō Imperial Palace|ja|仙洞御所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sentō Imperial Palace|qq=}}, the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the {{ill|Kyoto Machibugyō|ja|京都町奉行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Machibugyō|qq=}} offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|1=Five regent houses|2=ja|3=摂家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five regent houses|qq=}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgo-in Temple|ja|聖護院|1=Shōgo-in|lt=Shōgo-in Temple|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgo-in Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgo-in|qq=}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōren-in|qq=}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chion-in|qq=}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rōjū|qq=}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmei famine|qq=}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the {{ill|Kamo River|lt=Kamo River|lt=Kamo River (Yodo River system)|ja|鴨川 (淀川水系)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo River (Yodo River system)|qq=}} in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to {{ill|Gojō Street|ja|五条通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojō Street|qq=}} and then across the river to {{ill|Teramachi Street|ja|寺町通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teramachi Street|qq=}}, engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as {{ill|Kawaramachi Street|ja|河原町通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawaramachi Street|qq=}}, {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Street|ja|大宮通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Senbon Street|ja|千本通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senbon Street|qq=}}; and south to {{ill|Higashi Honganji|ja|東本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Honganji|qq=}}, {{ill|Nishi Honganji|ja|西本願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishi Honganji|qq=}}, and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the {{ill|Sentō Imperial Palace|ja|仙洞御所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sentō Imperial Palace|qq=}}, the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the {{ill|Kyoto Machibugyō|ja|京都町奉行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Machibugyō|qq=}} offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|1=Five regent houses|2=ja|3=摂家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five regent houses|qq=}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the {{ill|Shōgo-in Temple|ja|聖護院|1=Shōgo-in|lt=Shōgo-in Temple|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgo-in Temple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgo-in|qq=}} as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the {{ill|Shōren-in|ja|青蓮院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōren-in|qq=}}, while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at {{ill|Chion-in|ja|知恩院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chion-in|qq=}}. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior {{ill|Rōjū|ja|老中|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rōjū|qq=}} {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the {{ill|Tenmei famine|ja|天明の大飢饉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmei famine|qq=}}, funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|1=Five regent houses|2=ja|3=摂家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five regent houses|qq=}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:江戸時代の京都
revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|1=Five regent houses|2=ja|3=摂家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five regent houses|qq=}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|1=Five regent houses|2=ja|3=摂家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five regent houses|qq=}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the {{ill|Five regent houses|ja|摂関家|1=Five regent houses|2=ja|3=摂家|Five regent houses=Five regent houses|lt=Sesshō and Kampaku families|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five regent houses|qq=}}. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

<!--Tenmei Fire--><!--Tenmei Fire--> [[Category:1788 in Japan]] [[Category:江戸時代の京都]] [[Category:江戸時代の火災]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]] [[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]] [[zh:天明大火]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Fires in the Edo period
revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.adthree.com/NPO/tenmeitaika.html Map of Tenmei Fire Burn Area] (NPO Cultural Property Disaster Protection Society)
{{translated page|ja|天明の大火|version=104011964|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>
