revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. == Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[Nijō Castle]], and the [[Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>

By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.

The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.

Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

<!--Tenmei Fire--><!--Tenmei Fire--> [[Category:1788 in Japan]] [[Category:江戸時代の京都]] [[Category:江戸時代の火災]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]] [[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]] [[zh:天明大火]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]], and the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links == * [http://www.adthree.com/NPO/tenmeitaika.html Map of Tenmei Fire Burn Area] (NPO Cultural Property Disaster Protection Society)

The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11442674]] Wikidata name: Great Fire of Tenmei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]], and the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|qq=|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通|1=Kiyamachi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6418956}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通|1=Yamatoōji Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433430}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=|1=Kuramaguchi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11663902}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=|1=Chiekōin Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514774}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].

Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.

Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通|1=Kiyamachi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6418956}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通|1=Yamatoōji Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433430}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|qq=|1=Kuramaguchi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11663902}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|qq=|1=Chiekōin Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514774}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|qq=|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

<!--Tenmei Fire--><!--Tenmei Fire--> [[Category:1788 in Japan]] [[Category:Kyoto in the Edo period]] [[Category:Fires in the Edo period]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]] [[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]] [[zh:天明大火]] {{wikidata link|Q11442674}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天明の大火]][[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]][[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]][[zh:天明大火]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11442674]] Wikidata name: Great Fire of Tenmei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]], and the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通|1=Kiyamachi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6418956}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通|1=Yamatoōji Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433430}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|1=Kuramaguchi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11663902}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|1=Chiekōin Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514774}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.

The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天明の大火]][[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]][[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]][[zh:天明大火]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11442674]] Wikidata name: Great Fire of Tenmei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]], and the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通|1=Kiyamachi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6418956}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通|1=Yamatoōji Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433430}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|1=Kuramaguchi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11663902}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|1=Chiekōin Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514774}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天明の大火]][[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]][[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]][[zh:天明大火]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11442674]] Wikidata name: Great Fire of Tenmei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]], and the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通|1=Kiyamachi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6418956}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通|1=Yamatoōji Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433430}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|1=Kuramaguchi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11663902}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|1=Chiekōin Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514774}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

<!--Tenmei Fire--><!--Tenmei Fire--> [[Category:1788 in Japan]] [[Category:Kyoto in the Edo period]] [[Category:Fires in the Edo period]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]] [[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]] [[zh:天明大火]] {{wikidata link|Q11442674}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
{{wikidata link|Q11442674}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天明の大火]][[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]][[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]][[zh:天明大火]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11442674]] Wikidata name: Great Fire of Tenmei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]], and the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通|1=Kiyamachi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6418956}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通|1=Yamatoōji Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433430}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|1=Kuramaguchi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11663902}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|1=Chiekōin Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514774}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天明の大火]][[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]][[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]][[zh:天明大火]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11442674]] Wikidata name: Great Fire of Tenmei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]], and the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通|1=Kiyamachi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6418956}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通|1=Yamatoōji Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433430}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|1=Kuramaguchi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11663902}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|1=Chiekōin Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514774}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天明の大火]][[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]][[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]][[zh:天明大火]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天明の大火]][[ru:Большой пожар в Киото]][[uk:Велика кіотська пожежа]][[zh:天明大火]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11442674]] Wikidata name: Great Fire of Tenmei [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ページ番号|date=2015th year 10月}} '''Tenmei no Taika''' (天明の大火) refers to a [[:en:Fire|Fire]] that occurred in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] on {{nihongo|January 30, Tenmei 8|天明8th year 1月30日|1788th year }} (March 7, 1788, in the [[:en:Gregorian calendar|Gregorian calendar]]). Known as '''Donguri-yake''' (団栗焼け) after the name of the location where it originated, it is also called the '''Saru-doshi no Taika''' (申年の大火, "Great Fire of the Year of the Monkey") based on the Chinese zodiac. Simply calling it the '''Kyoto Great Fire''' (京都大火) or '''Miyako-yake''' (都焼け, "Burning of the Capital") usually refers to this specific event.
It was the largest fire in Kyoto's history, destroying the [[Kyoto Imperial Palace|Imperial Palace]], [[:en:Nijō Castle|Nijō Castle]], and the [[:en:Kyoto Shoshidai|Kyoto Shoshidai]] along with over 80% of the city. The damage surpassed the devastation caused by the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and left a lasting impact on Kyoto's economy. Together with the [[Hōei no Taika]] and the [[Dondon-yake]] during the [[:en:Genji era|Genji era]], these are sometimes referred to as Kyoto's "Three Great Fires."<ref>''{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=Kokushi Daijiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6426904}}'', Volume 9 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1988), entry "Tenmei no Kyoto Taika" (author: Katsuhisa Moriya).</ref>
== Overview == The fire broke out early in the morning of January 30 (March 7), originating from a [[:en:row house|row house]] in {{nihongo|Miyagawa Town Donguri Zushi|宮川町団栗辻子|Miyagawa-chō Dōnguri Zushi}}, east of the [[:en:Kamo River|Kamo River (Yodo River system)]] in present-day {{ill|Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja|京都市東山区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was reportedly an arson attack on a vacant house. Strong winds quickly spread the fire south to [[:en:Gojō Street|Gojō Street]] and then across the river to [[:en:Teramachi Street|Teramachi Street]], engulfing the city center. By the evening, the flames reached Nijō Castle's inner keep and continued north to the Imperial Palace. The fire was finally extinguished early on February 2 (March 9), two days after it began.
The fire spread as far east as [[:en:Kawaramachi Street|Kawaramachi Street]], {{ill|Kiyamachi Street|ja|木屋町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyamachi Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木屋町通|1=Kiyamachi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6418956}}, and {{ill|Yamato Ōji Street|ja|大和大路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Ōji Street|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和大路通|1=Yamatoōji Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433430}}; north to {{ill|Kamigoryō Shrine|ja|上御霊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigoryō Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Kuramaguchi Street|ja|鞍馬口通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramaguchi Street|1=Kuramaguchi Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11663902}}, and {{ill|Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja|今宮御旅所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imamiya Shrine Temporary Worship Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; west to {{ill|Chiekōin Street|ja|智恵光院通|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chiekōin Street|1=Chiekōin Street|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11514774}}, [[:en:Ōmiya Street|Ōmiya Street]], and [[:en:Senbon Street|Senbon Street]]; and south to [[:en:Higashi Hongan-ji|Higashi Honganji]], [[:en:Nishi Hongan-ji|Nishi Honganji]], and Rokujō Street. The destruction included not only the Imperial Palace and Nijō Castle but also the [[:en:Sentō Imperial Palace|Sentō Imperial Palace]], the Kyoto Shoshidai residence, the [[:en:Kyoto machi-bugyō|Kyoto Machibugyō]] offices, and the mansions of the [[:en:Five regent houses|Sesshō and Kampaku families]]. Official records from the Kyoto [[Chōdai]] (町代) estimate the damage as follows: 1,424 out of 1,967 city wards burned, 36,797 houses destroyed, 65,340 households affected, 201 temples, 37 shrines lost, and 150 deaths. However, some suspect the death toll was understated, with estimates suggesting as many as 1,800 casualties.
[[:en:Emperor Kōkaku|Emperor Kōkaku]] lived at the [[:en:Shōgo-in|Shōgo-in Temple]] as a temporary palace for three years until the Imperial Palace was rebuilt. {{nihongo|Dowager Empress Go-Sakuramachi|後桜町上皇}} moved to the [[:en:Shōren-in|Shōren-in]], while her mother, Seiki Mon’in, took up residence at [[:en:Chion-in|Chion-in]]. To facilitate travel between Chion-in and Shōren-in, a corridor was built by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]].
Shocked by the disaster, the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] dispatched senior [[:en:Rōjū|Rōjū]] {{nihongo|Matsudaira Sadanobu|松平定信}} to Kyoto to discuss recovery measures with the Imperial Court. Shortly afterward, {{nihongo|The Illustrated Commentary on the Imperial Palace|大内裏図考證}}, authored by Uramatsu Kozan, was completed. The reconstruction of the Imperial Palace followed its traditional style based on these studies. Despite the shogunate's chronic financial difficulties and public suffering during the [[:en:Great Tenmei famine|Tenmei famine]], funding was provided for the project. Matsudaira Sadanobu, who opposed extravagant rebuilding, later ordered Kyoto officials to reject new demands from the court, leading to tensions and eventually contributing to disputes such as the {{ill|Respectful Title Incident|ja|尊号一件|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Respectful Title Incident|1=Respectful Title Incident|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458659}}.
Ten years after the fire, on {{nihongo|July 1, Kansei 10|寛政10th year 7月1日|1798}}, the {{ill|Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja|方広寺大仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-ji Daibutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Great Buddha of Kyoto) and its hall were destroyed by lightning-induced fire. Although this fire threatened to cause another city-wide disaster like the Tenmei fire, it miraculously did not spread beyond the vicinity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=村山修一|title=京都大仏御殿盛衰記|publisher=法藏館|year=2003}}</ref>
